core_intuitions: Decode step-time is FLAT below the arithmetic-intensity breakeven B* (weights stream from HBM once per step regardless of batch), then rises linearly above it — so every extra request packed below B* produces a free token at zero added latency; utilization, not raw hardware, is the lever.

Cost per million output tokens is inversely proportional to sustained throughput: $/1M = R*1e6/(T*3600). Because throughput saturates with batch size, the cost curve is a hyperbola — running a half-empty GPU multiplies cost several-fold (the chapter's worked example: batch 4 -> ~$10, batch 32 -> ~$3).

Output tokens cost 3-5x more than input tokens NOT because of FLOPs (both are ~2P per token) but because of bandwidth: each decode token pays for its own full weight read from HBM, while parallel prefill amortizes one weight read across the whole prompt.
The latency-throughput-cost triangle: you cannot optimize all three; the job is to sit at max utilization subject to a latency SLO.
KV cache is a first-class resource whose per-sequence footprint grows with context length, capping how many concurrent sequences fit and thus throughput/cost.
Quantization (FP8/INT8/INT4) proportionally cuts the bytes streamed per decode step, directly shrinking the bandwidth floor and the GPU count.

new_figures.name: econ-decode-step-time-knee
new_figures.concept: Decode step-time vs batch size: a flat bandwidth-bound floor until B*, then a linear compute-bound ramp — every request below B* is a free token.
new_figures.rationale: This is the mechanistic heart of the whole chapter's economics. The prose, the code, and the code output all circle this one shape (flat ~42 ms floor to B*~295, then linear), but a reader must SEE 'the line is flat, so adding requests costs zero latency but yields more tokens' for the cost intuition to click. It is distinct from the other-chapter roofline number line (which plots arithmetic intensity, not wall-clock step time vs batch) and from the throughput-TPOT frontier (which plots throughput vs latency). This plots the single quantity the scheduler controls (batch) against the single quantity it pays (step time).
new_figures.placement: Latency SLOs in Practice (right after the code block and its 'Below B* ... flat at ~42 ms per step' paragraph)
new_figures.brief: A single line chart, viewBox about 860x430. X-axis: 'active batch size B (concurrent decoding sequences)', ticks at 1, and a marked position for B* (label 'B* = FLOP/s / BW ~ 295'). Y-axis: 'decode step time (ms per token)'. Draw a piecewise curve: a perfectly HORIZONTAL segment from small batch up to B* at a constant height labelled 'bandwidth-bound floor: weights stream from HBM once per step (~42 ms)', then a straight LINEAR ramp rising to the right past B* labelled 'compute-bound: step time grows linearly with batch'. Put a vertical dashed guideline at B* (use var(--warn) accent) from axis to the knee, labelled 'the knee / breakeven B*'. In the flat region, stack 3-4 small stick-figure or dot 'request' glyphs sitting UNDER the flat line with a bracket and the callout 'each added request here = +1 free token, +0 latency'. In the ramp region, a callout 'past B*: each added request adds latency, no extra tokens/dollar'. Shade the flat region faintly with var(--good) tint (the win zone) and the ramp region faintly with var(--warn) tint. Small footnote: 'illustrative: floor height and B* depend on model size and GPU (here 70B BF16 on one H100)'. Theme-safe: use var(--ink-soft), var(--accent), var(--good), var(--warn), var(--border-2) with light/dark-safe fallbacks; class hooks v-stroke/v-grid/v-accent acceptable. All labels inside the viewBox, no overlaps; the FLAT-then-LINEAR shape must be unmistakable at a glance. Figcaption should state: below B* decode is bandwidth-bound and step time is constant, so packing more concurrent sequences is a pure throughput win at zero latency cost; above B* you pay latency for nothing.

new_figures.name: econ-cost-per-token-hyperbola
new_figures.concept: Dollars per million output tokens collapses as sustained throughput (utilization) rises — a hyperbola, with the chapter's two worked operating points marked.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's central economic claim ('low utilization is the enemy of cost efficiency') is delivered as two algebra steps that most readers will not internalize. Plotting $/1M vs throughput as the hyperbola $/1M = R*1e6/(T*3600), and dropping the chapter's own two points (batch 4 -> ~$10/1M at ~550 tok/s, batch 32 -> ~$3/1M at ~1800 tok/s), makes 'same GPU, same rent, throughput is the only knob' visceral. This is the payoff figure that connects the B* knee to dollars.
new_figures.placement: The Math of Dollars Per Million Tokens (immediately after the 'Worked Example: H100 cluster serving Llama-3 70B' admonition)
new_figures.brief: A single decaying-hyperbola line chart, viewBox about 860x440. X-axis: 'sustained output throughput (tokens/s per node)'. Y-axis: 'cost per 1M output tokens ($)'. Draw the smooth 1/T hyperbola falling steeply then flattening as throughput grows. Plot TWO labelled points taken from the chapter's worked example: (a) low-traffic point at ~550 tok/s / ~$10 per 1M, callout 'batch 4 (idle GPU): ~$10 / 1M' in a var(--warn) chip; (b) busy point at ~1800 tok/s / ~$3 per 1M, callout 'batch 32 (saturated): ~$3 / 1M' in a var(--good) chip. Connect the two points with a faint vertical/horizontal delta bracket annotated '~1/8 the traffic -> ~3x the cost per token'. Anchor the formula as a boxed equation on the chart: '$/1M = R x 1e6 / (T x 3600), rent R fixed'. Emphasize that the x-position is set by batch/utilization by adding a thin secondary tick strip under the x-axis showing batch grows left-to-right toward B*. Shade high-throughput end faintly var(--good) ('cost-efficient / high utilization'), low-throughput end faintly var(--warn) ('half-empty GPU: expensive'). Footnote: 'rent per hour is constant; only throughput moves you along the curve. Numbers illustrative (4xH100 node, ~$20/hr).' Theme-safe var() usage with fallbacks, all text inside viewBox, no overlaps, clear reading order left(expensive)->right(cheap). Figcaption: because rent is fixed, cost per token is purely the inverse of sustained throughput; running below saturation slides you up the steep part of the hyperbola, which is why low utilization multiplies cost.

new_figures.name: econ-output-token-weight-read-toll
new_figures.concept: Why output tokens cost more than input tokens: prefill reads the weights ONCE and amortizes across the whole prompt, while decode pays a full weight read per single emitted token.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter states, repeats, and boxes in Key Takeaways that output tokens cost 3-5x input tokens because of BANDWIDTH not FLOPs. This is the most commonly misunderstood point in inference economics (people assume it's more math). A side-by-side 'one weight read amortized over many prompt tokens' vs 'one weight read per output token' visual makes the asymmetry undeniable. It complements rather than duplicates the other chapter's arithmetic-intensity number line: here the framing is explicitly economic (a per-token 'toll' = one HBM weight sweep), tying to dollars.
new_figures.placement: Input Tokens vs. Output Tokens (right after the paragraph explaining decode reads the entire weight matrix per token)
new_figures.brief: A two-panel side-by-side comparison, viewBox about 880x400. LEFT panel titled 'PREFILL (input tokens)': show a single large block labelled 'model weights' with ONE bold arrow sweeping out of HBM, feeding a ROW of many prompt-token boxes processed together (draw ~6 small token squares in one pass). Caption under it: 'one weight read amortized across the whole prompt -> near-peak FLOP utilization -> cheap per token'. RIGHT panel titled 'DECODE (output tokens)': show a vertical sequence of steps t1, t2, t3, ...; at EACH step draw a full weight-read arrow out of the same 'model weights' block feeding a SINGLE token box. Repeat the weights-read arrow at every step so the reader sees the weights streamed over and over. Caption: 'one full weight read per emitted token -> memory-bound, low utilization -> expensive per token'. Between panels, a bold takeaway strip: 'same FLOPs per token (~2P) both sides -- the gap is bandwidth: each output token pays its own weight-read toll'. Use a small 'toll booth' or repeated-arrow motif to convey the per-token cost. Keep counts as small illustrative integers (6 prompt tokens, 3-4 decode steps). Theme-safe var(--accent) for the decode/expensive side, var(--good) or var(--accent-2) for the prefill/cheap side, neutral var(--surface)/var(--border-2) for the weight block; all with fallbacks. Everything inside viewBox, panels clearly separated, no overlapping arrows. Figcaption: prefill amortizes a single HBM weight sweep across every prompt token at near-peak utilization, whereas decode re-streams the entire weight matrix to emit each single token; identical per-token FLOPs, radically different bandwidth cost — this is why providers price output tokens several times higher than input tokens.

summary: This capstone economics chapter is concept-rich but had ZERO figures. Its distinct economic intuitions are not served by the roofline/KV figures that live in sibling chapters. I propose three disciplined STATIC infographics that make its core mechanisms click: (1) econ-decode-step-time-knee -- the flat-then-linear step-time-vs-batch curve showing every request below B* is a free token (the mechanistic heart); (2) econ-cost-per-token-hyperbola -- $/1M output tokens as the inverse of sustained throughput, with the chapter's own two worked operating points, making 'low utilization is the enemy of cost' visceral (the economic payoff); (3) econ-output-token-weight-read-toll -- the prefill-amortizes-once vs decode-reads-per-token asymmetry that explains why output tokens cost 3-5x, a point the chapter states three times. No animation is warranted: each idea is a single static frame. All briefs stay theme-safe, use only the chapter's own illustrative integers, and fit inside their viewBoxes.
